name: idk-widk
description: A Codex plugin for people who do not know what they do not know: it routes prompts into the right idkWidk workflow before action, turns messy prompts into engineering briefs, keeps Mission Control, reconstructs old behavior after regressions, researches open-source solutions, runs source repos in ignored workspaces, requires Chrome DevTools MCP connected to Chrome for Testing on http://127.0.0.1:9222 for agent browser UI runtime evidence, performs visual QA with screenshots and parity checks, guides license-aware feature adoption, supports clean-room feature parity, prevents scope drift, and verifies implementation.

interface.shortDescription: I do not know what I do not know: senior engineering and Chrome for Testing visual QA guidance for AI-assisted builders.
interface.longDescription: idkWidk helps non-engineers and AI-assisted builders turn messy prompts, screenshots, bugs, ideas, fears, constraints, and GitHub repos into clear engineering work. It classifies the task, preserves the original objective, reconstructs historical behavior when regressions happen, extracts behavior contracts from evidence, researches GitHub and open-source projects when useful, runs source repos in ignored workspaces for runtime observation, requires Chrome DevTools MCP connected to Chrome for Testing on http://127.0.0.1:9222 for web UI runtime labs, captures screenshots and visual QA evidence from that testing browser, reuses existing tabs instead of opening duplicates, guides license-aware feature adoption for permissive repositories, supports clean-room feature parity when copying is unsafe, avoids side-quest drift, prevents silent refactors, and verifies changes before calling them done.
defaultPrompt: Use idkWidk Routing Gate. Before any action, response, file read, shell command, browser action, code edit, or clarification question, decide which idkWidk workflow applies, then activate that workflow first.

Use idkWidk. I am not a software engineer. Treat my message as messy raw input, classify the task, extract the real goal, inspect any screenshots or files, and do not start coding until the evidence and next safe step are clear.
Use idkWidk Intake Interpreter. Turn my rambling message, attachments, screenshots, fears, guesses, and constraints into an engineering brief, then recommend the next best action.
Use idkWidk Mission Control. Keep the original objective visible, classify blockers versus side quests, and create or update Mission Control for multi-step work.

Use idkWidk Open Source Intelligence. Search for relevant current GitHub repos and examples, inspect actual source files instead of only the README, check license compatibility, and recommend the fastest safe integration strategy.

Use idkWidk Behavior Archaeology. I had this working before and it changed. Reconstruct the old behavior from evidence, compare it to current behavior, decide what to keep, fix, ignore, or mark unknown, then turn only the chosen behavior into tests.

Use idkWidk Regression Analysis. Find where behavior changed, inspect old and current code, build an evidence table, extract the desired contract, and verify the fix with characterization, golden master, or acceptance tests as appropriate.
Use idkWidk to manage this bug, feature, refactor, or release. Choose the lightest safe process, create only the needed artifacts, and clearly state what was verified and what remains unverified.

Use idkWidk Permissive OSS Feature Adoption. If the repo license permits reuse, extract the feature with one-to-one behavior and visual parity where appropriate, preserve notices, use the smallest safe integration strategy, and verify parity with acceptance, visual, DOM, and behavior tests.

Use idkWidk Clean Room Reimplementation. If the license is restrictive, unclear, missing, or incompatible, do not copy source, assets, CSS, text, or unique UI expression. Create a behavior-only spec, isolate contaminated source review from implementation, and build functional parity from the clean spec with project-owned implementation.

Use idkWidk Visual Runtime QA. For any browser-visible, UI, Chrome extension, or visual parity work, use Chrome DevTools MCP connected to Chrome for Testing as the required runtime inspection tool, capture source and target screenshots, inspect DOM/CSS/console/network, and do not claim parity or completion without visual evidence.

Use idkWidk External Repo Runtime Lab with mandatory visual QA. Clone the source repo into .idkwidk/external-repos, scan for screenshots/GIFs/videos, run the app safely, capture runtime screenshots, DOM/CSS evidence, console checks, and network checks, then compare against my implementation before calling the work done.

Use idkWidk Chrome for Testing Visual QA. For any browser UI, feature parity, external repo, or visual regression task, require Chrome DevTools MCP connected to Chrome for Testing at http://127.0.0.1:9222, reuse existing tabs, capture baseline screenshots before changes, capture after screenshots after changes, compare them, and do not claim visual parity without evidence.

Use idkWidk UI setup validation. Before extracting a feature from an external repo, prove the source app is actually running in the intended state by comparing runtime screenshots against the repo README, docs, demo GIFs, screenshots, or expected feature inventory.

Use idkWidk Visual Runtime QA. For any browser UI, visual parity, external repo feature extraction, or design-sensitive change, use Chrome DevTools MCP and screenshot evidence. Capture source baseline, candidate before, candidate after, DOM/CSS/runtime evidence, and do not claim visual parity without a before/after comparison.

Use idkWidk External Repo Runtime Lab with mandatory Chrome DevTools. Clone the repo into .idkwidk/external-repos, inspect visual assets, run the real app, verify setup health, capture screenshots, DOM/CSS evidence, console checks, and network checks, and stop if the app looks obviously simpler or different from its README/demo assets.

Use idkWidk Visual Runtime QA. For this web UI or source-repo parity task, require Chrome DevTools MCP, capture baseline screenshots, inspect DOM/CSS/network/console evidence, compare before and after, and do not call the work done without visual evidence.

Use idkWidk External Repo Runtime Lab. Clone this repo into the ignored .idkwidk/external-repos workspace, run it safely, use mandatory Chrome DevTools MCP for web runtime inspection, scan for README images/GIFs/videos, capture source screenshots, and diagnose setup problems before copying or reimplementing anything.
Use idkWidk Visual Parity. Compare the upstream feature and my implementation with screenshots, DOM/CSS observations, responsive viewport checks, and a visual parity matrix before declaring parity.

Use idkWidk Chrome for Testing Visual QA. For browser-visible work, use only Chrome DevTools MCP connected to Chrome for Testing at http://127.0.0.1:9222. Reuse an existing matching tab, do not open duplicate tabs, capture screenshots, inspect DOM/CSS/console/network, and do not call the UI verified without evidence.

Use idkWidk External Repo Runtime Lab with Chrome for Testing. Clone the repo into .idkwidk/external-repos, run it safely, connect Chrome DevTools MCP to Chrome for Testing on port 9222, reuse tabs instead of creating duplicates, capture source screenshots and DOM/CSS evidence, then compare against my implementation.
